message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 9 (all tests complete — empty, underpopulation x2, survival, overpopulation, reproduction, blinker, block, negative coordinates)
Recent changes: Tests 7-9 (blinker, block, negative coordinates) all passed against the existing full implementation with no code changes. The implementation is complete: builds a candidate Map of live cells + dead neighbors, applies survives (2-3) and isBorn (exactly 3) predicates, uses key/countLiveNeighbors/NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS helpers.

This is the final refactor. Do a holistic review of the complete implementation while keeping all 9 tests green.
EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously. This is the last test in the list.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 9 tests green. Now the holistic refactor review.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing every named element against its actual purpose:

- `Cell` — type alias `[number, number]` with `// [x, y]` comment. Clear, reveals intent.
- `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS` — the 8 surrounding deltas. Accurate and descriptive.
- `key` — converts a Cell to a string identity for Set/Map. Concise and clear.
- `countLiveNeighbors` — counts live neighbors of a cell. Exactly describes what it does.
- `survives` — predicate for a live cell staying alive (2-3). Clear.
- `isBorn` — predicate for a dead cell coming to life (exactly 3). Clear.
- `nextGeneration` — computes the next generation. Clear, the public API.
- Locals: `live`, `candidates`, `neighbor`, `liveNeighbors` — all reveal intent.

**Naming decision:** All names already clearly reveal intent. No rename needed.

## Step 2: Initial APP Mass (focus on `nextGeneration`, lines 21-37)

- Bindings: `cells`, `live`, `candidates`, `cell`, `dx`, `dy`, `neighbor`, `cell` (filter param), `liveNeighbors` = 9
- Constants: `[cell[0]+dx, cell[1]+dy]` literals indices, plus `live`/`isBorn` literals — counting literals `0`, `1` (in indexing) appear several times. Approx constants ≈ 4
- Invocations: `new Set`, `cells.map`, `new Map`, `candidates.set` (x2), `key` (multiple: line 25, 28, 32, 33), `[...].values`, `.filter`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `survives`, `isBorn`, `live.has` = ~13 invocations × 2 = 26
- Conditionals: 1 ternary × 4 = 4
- Loops: 2 `for` loops × 5 = 10
- Assignments: `candidates.set` mutations are invocations already counted

The dominant cost is the **two-loop candidate building** plus a separate ternary lookup that re-computes `key(cell)` and `live.has(key(cell))` — `key(cell)` is computed redundantly (line 32 via `countLiveNeighbors` internals are separate, but line 33 calls `key(cell)` again, and line 28/25 build keys).

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules in priority order

**Rule 1 (Tests pass):** Green. ✅

**Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** Strong already. One minor observation: in the filter, `key(cell)` is called twice conceptually (once inside `countLiveNeighbors` indirectly — no, that uses neighbors; but `live.has(key(cell))` on line 33 recomputes the cell's own key). This is a small readability/duplication point, not a clarity defect.

**Rule 3 (No duplication):** The candidate-building loop does `candidates.set(key(neighbor), neighbor)` and separately `candidates.set(key(cell), cell)`. The live cell is also one of the candidates it would naturally be added — but it's explicitly added to guarantee inclusion (a live cell with 0 neighbors must still be considered). There is a genuine small duplication: `key(cell)` computed on line 25, then again on line 33. Minor.

**Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** No unnecessary abstractions. Every helper earns its place (`key`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `survives`, `isBorn` are all used and each clarifies a distinct concept).

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

I'll make one targeted improvement: extract the per-candidate liveness rule into a well-named predicate `isAliveNextGeneration`. This removes the inline ternary from the orchestration function, separates the *rules* (which combine `survives`/`isBorn`) from the *mechanics* (candidate collection), and improves Rule 2 (intent) and Rule 4 (the main function reads as a pipeline). The `key(cell)` for the live check moves into the focused predicate where it reads naturally.

content.text:
All 9 tests still green and the type check passes (no output = clean).

## Step 5: APP Mass — `nextGeneration` before vs. after

The orchestration function `nextGeneration` was carrying loops + a ternary inline. After extraction:

**Before (`nextGeneration` body):**
- Bindings: `live`, `candidates`, `cell`, `dx`, `dy`, `neighbor`, `cell`(filter), `liveNeighbors` ≈ 8
- Invocations: `new Set`, `.map`, `new Map`, `.set`×2, `key`×3+, `.values`, `.filter`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `survives`, `isBorn`, `live.has` ≈ 13 × 2 = 26
- Conditionals: 1 ternary × 4 = 4
- Loops: 2 × 5 = 10
- **Approx total: ~48**

**After (`nextGeneration` body):**
- Bindings: `live`, `cell` ≈ 2
- Invocations: `new Set`, `.map`, `candidateCells`, `.filter`, `isAliveNextGeneration` = 5 × 2 = 10
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0 (moved into `candidateCells`)
- **Approx total: ~12**

The main function's mass dropped from ~48 to ~12. The total mass across the module is essentially unchanged (the loops/ternary moved into focused helpers), which is the desired outcome — Rule 2 (intent) improved without Rule 4 cost. The two new functions each have low, focused mass and a single responsibility.

## Step 6: Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- ✅ Rule 2 (Reveals intent): `nextGeneration` now reads as a clear pipeline — "compute the live set, then keep the candidate cells that are alive next generation." The two distinct concerns (which cells to consider vs. which survive) are now named: `candidateCells` and `isAliveNextGeneration`.
- ✅ Rule 4 (Fewest elements at each level): the orchestration function is now minimal; complexity lives in small, single-purpose helpers rather than one dense function.
- ✅ APP: `nextGeneration` mass ~48 → ~12; module total roughly unchanged (no added complexity, just better distributed).
- ❌ Naming: no renames needed — existing names already revealed intent.
- ❌ Duplication: no remaining meaningful duplication.

## Step 7: Completion

Final implementation at `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_17-35-47_game-of-life-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`:

```typescript
export function nextGeneration(cells: Cell[]): Cell[] {
  const live = new Set(cells.map(key));
  return candidateCells(cells).filter((cell) =>
    isAliveNextGeneration(cell, live)
  );
}
```

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Extracted `candidateCells` and `isAliveNextGeneration`; `nextGeneration` is now a clear two-step pipeline.
- **Mass Change**: `nextGeneration` ~48 → ~12; module total roughly unchanged (complexity redistributed into focused helpers, not added).
- **Tests**: All 9 passing ✅; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

This was the final test in the list. Per experiment mode, writing the done marker.

content.text:
Final refactor complete. Summary of the holistic review:

**Naming (first priority):** All names already revealed intent — `Cell`, `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS`, `key`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `survives`, `isBorn`, `nextGeneration`. No renames needed.

**Refactoring applied:** Extracted two focused helpers from the monolithic `nextGeneration`:
- `candidateCells(cells)` — builds the deduplicated set of live cells plus their dead neighbors (the cells worth evaluating).
- `isAliveNextGeneration(cell, live)` — the survival/birth rule predicate combining `survives` and `isBorn`.

`nextGeneration` now reads as a clear pipeline: build the live set, then keep the candidate cells alive next generation.

**Simple Design Rules:**
- Rule 1 (tests pass): all 9 green before and after.
- Rule 2 (reveals intent): improved — orchestration vs. rules vs. candidate-collection are now separately named.
- Rule 3 (no duplication): none remaining.
- Rule 4 (fewest elements): main function minimized; complexity lives in small single-purpose helpers, none of them gratuitous.

**APP mass:** `nextGeneration` body dropped from ~48 to ~12; module total roughly unchanged (loops and the ternary were relocated into focused helpers, not added). Rule 2 gain with no Rule 4 cost.

**Verification:** `pnpm test` → 9 passed; `tsc --noEmit` clean.

This was the last test in the list, so I wrote the done marker.

- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_17-35-47_game-of-life-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`
